summary: Gruber predicts Apple will name its 2019 iPhone lineup 'iPhone 11 / 11 Pro / 11 Pro Max,' and explains why this scheme makes more sense than dropping numbers entirely. He argues that Apple's strategy of keeping older iPhone models in production at lower price points — unlike MacBooks and iPads which simply replace predecessors — necessitates unique numbered names for each generation. He makes the case that calling the XR successor 'iPhone 11' (without Pro) would correctly signal it as the default iPhone most people should buy. He also defends 'Pro' as appropriate for iPhones while conceding 'Pro Max' sounds like a nutritional supplement.
key_points: Apple's practice of keeping older iPhones in production at lower price points for years makes numberless naming impractical, unlike MacBooks and iPads which replace predecessors outright.
Calling the XR successor simply 'iPhone 11' would correctly brand it as the default iPhone for most buyers, given its superior battery life, fun colors, and lower price.
'Pro' in Apple's marketing doesn't always mean professional — sometimes it just means premium, and iPhones are genuinely used as professional tools.
Gruber acknowledges his track record on iPhone naming predictions is terrible, comparing himself to a stopped clock that's right twice a day.
'Pro Max' as a name sounds like a nutrition supplement for weightlifters, and Apple would likely advertise both Pro sizes under one 'iPhone 11 Pro' umbrella anyway.
money_quotes: Anyone who's been betting based on my iPhone naming predictions is deep in the hole at this point.
Nice and clean. And it would do away with the ungainliness of ever-incrementing integers in product names. Are we really going to have an 'iPhone 19' at some point?
Older models hang around — in production — for years. Apple just slots them into lower price points as they get older.
By calling the new XR the just plain 'iPhone 11', it would implicitly establish the new XR model as the default iPhone, the iPhone most people should buy.
Fun colors, lower price, longer battery life — that's all you need to say.
'Pro', in Apple product marketing parlance, does mean professional sometimes (e.g. Mac Pro and iMac Pro). But sometimes it just means premium.
'Pro Max' sounds a bit like a vitamin supplement for weightlifters, frankly.
key_insight: Apple's unique strategy of keeping older iPhones in production at lower price points forces them to use numbered naming schemes that other Apple product lines can avoid.
